PrintCSS Test Document

Demonstrating Paged Media Features

Generated by HTML2PDF

2024

Table of Contents

  • Introduction 1
  • Typography Features 1
  • Tables 2
  • Lists 2
  • Conclusion 3

1. Introduction

This document demonstrates various PrintCSS and CSS Paged Media features. These features allow for sophisticated control over how web content is rendered for print output, including page breaks, running headers and footers, and page-specific styling.

The @page rule is the cornerstone of CSS Paged Media, allowing you to define the page box, margins, and various page selectors for different types of pages within your document.

2. Typography Features

2.1 Widows and Orphans Control

CSS Paged Media provides properties to control widows (lines at the top of a page) and orphans (lines at the bottom of a page). The paragraphs in this document are configured to require at least three lines, preventing awkward page breaks that leave single lines isolated.

When a paragraph spans multiple pages, these properties ensure that the break occurs at an appropriate location, maintaining readability and visual appeal. This is particularly important for longer documents such as books, reports, and academic papers.

2.2 Page Break Control

Headings in this document use page-break-after: avoid to prevent them from appearing at the bottom of a page without any following content. This ensures that headings always have at least some content beneath them.

Similarly, tables and figures use page-break-inside: avoid to prevent them from being split across pages whenever possible. This maintains the integrity of data presentations and visual elements.

3. Tables

Tables are common elements in documents that require special consideration for print. The following table demonstrates proper table formatting with borders, padding, and background colors.

Table 1: Sample Data
Item Description Quantity Price
A001 Standard Widget 100 $25.00
A002 Premium Widget 50 $50.00
A003 Deluxe Widget 25 $100.00

The table above should remain intact on a single page if space permits, thanks to the page-break-inside: avoid property.

4. Lists

Lists are another important element in document structure. Here are examples of both ordered and unordered lists:

4.1 Unordered List

  • First item in the list with some additional text to demonstrate how list items wrap when they contain longer content.
  • Second item in the list
  • Third item in the list
  • Fourth item in the list

4.2 Ordered List

  2. Second step in the process with more detailed explanation of what needs to be done.
  3. Third step in the process
  4. Fourth step in the process

5. Conclusion

This document has demonstrated several key features of CSS Paged Media:

  • Page setup with @page rules
  • Running headers and footers
  • Page break control
  • Widows and orphans control
  • Named pages for different sections
  • Print-specific styling

These features enable the creation of professional-quality print documents from HTML and CSS, bridging the gap between web content and traditional publishing formats.

For more information about CSS Paged Media, refer to the W3C specification and browser documentation for the latest support information.
